primary_reason: The paper explains and demonstrates selection bias in a common causal epidemiological analysis.
The study applies a spatially explicit agent-based transmission model to compare indoor masking, movement, distancing, gathering-size, and ventilation interventions for SARS-CoV-2.
The study demonstrates a transferable audit design that benchmarks diagnosis, microbiology, antibiotic treatment, and surgery for diabetic foot infections against clinical guidelines.

The study applies virtual screening and molecular-dynamics procedures to nominate SARS-CoV-2 protease inhibitors but does not contribute a transferable epidemiologic design, population model, or surveillance method.
The record constructs a molecular antibody–antigen docking benchmark for structural prediction and therapeutic design without identifying a transferable infectious-disease epidemiology method.
The study fits a population pharmacokinetic model and uses Monte Carlo simulation to compare posaconazole dosing strategies for fungal-infection prophylaxis in immunocompromised children.
The study uses routine multicountry data to simulate how varying implementation and compliance with a bacterial-LRTI decision rule would change pediatric antibiotic prescribing.
The prospective network study demonstrates longitudinal antibiotic-use surveillance coupled with inter-ward benchmarking and feedback as an antimicrobial-stewardship evaluation design.

The paper develops and fits a multiscale mechanistic model that separates parasite development, mosquito survival, transmission, and observation processes when estimating malaria's extrinsic incubation period.

The paper develops a geographically and demographically parameterized local agent-based transmission framework with interactive software and demonstrates its use for timing COVID-19 quarantine policies.

The paper develops the transferable INFEKTA agent-based framework combining disease parameters, demography, mobility routines, place networks, and distancing policies, then demonstrates it for COVID-19 in Bogotá.
The study develops an abstract agent-based model coupling infection transmission with individual economic activity to examine how population-level health and economic trade-offs emerge.
The study applies an agent-based model with demographic and social-contact structure to examine quarantine, distancing, and transit reductions for COVID-19-like epidemic control.
The paper develops a location-adaptable hourly agent-based COVID-19 model, validates it against county data, and evaluates lockdown and contact-tracing scenarios.
The study constructs demographic benchmarks from a nationally representative survey to support measurement and monitoring of equity in COVID-19 vaccine coverage among priority populations.
Using an underrecruited pediatric urinary-infection trial as motivation, the study simulates trial scenarios to evaluate how Bayesian power-prior discounting affects small-sample inference.
This review explains transferable agent-based modeling approaches for polymicrobial communities and infection-related biofilms.

The study combines sensor-derived hospital contact data with a dynamic agent-based model to quantify how occupation, timing, transmission rates, and hand-hygiene compliance shape microorganism spread.
The study uses a hybrid agent-based and equation-based measles model to compare school-closure targeting based on outbreak proximity and network centrality.

The study applies docking and molecular-dynamics screening to nominate tea compounds against SARS-CoV-2 proteins but offers no transferable epidemiologic design or population-level infectious-disease method.
This tutorial develops and evaluates equation-learning methods for approximating stochastic agent-based infectious-disease models.
The study combines an analytical epidemic model, university data, and agent-based simulations to compare COVID-19 testing, tracing, and preventive reopening strategies for educational institutions.
This agent-based infectious-process simulation evaluates mobility restrictions and emergent superspreading behavior.
The study uses an agent-based social-network model to test how transmission overdispersion modifies the comparative effect of limiting repeated and nonrepeated contacts.

The study demonstrates a workplace SARS-CoV-2 surveillance and operational benchmarking framework integrating targeted and random testing, variant surveillance, contact tracing, case management, and return-to-work monitoring.

The record discovers and experimentally benchmarks bactericidal lysin enzymes for C. difficile therapy but does not provide a transferable epidemiologic, surveillance, or transmission-analysis method.
The study builds an agent-based model from four countries' policy and epidemic data to compare effects on COVID-19 cases, intensive-care occupancy, recovery, and mortality.
This agent-based model estimates vaccination effects on pneumococcal infections, antibiotic use, resistance, and costs.

The study develops a traveler microsimulation from SARS-CoV-2 natural-history data to compare pre-travel and post-travel testing and quarantine strategies using infectious days and case detection outcomes.
This abstract-free letter provides no supplied evidence of an original method, diagnostic analysis, correction, guidance framework, or reproducibility contribution.

The study diagnoses gaps in mandated healthcare-associated-infection reporting by comparing those measures with the CDC hospital-onset sepsis definition and validating missed events through record review.

The paper develops and validates an open-source, individual-resolution agent-based platform incorporating testing, treatment, vaccination, competing symptoms, and realistic population structure for small-area COVID-19 modeling.

The paper establishes a vetted Candida auris outbreak genome benchmark with concordant genomic, phylogenetic, and epidemiological evidence for reproducible validation and comparison of phylogenomic pipelines.
This agent-based model projects effects of incarceration and treatment policies on HIV-discordant partnerships.
This pedestrian agent simulation estimates contact exposure times for infectious-disease risk assessment.

The manikin simulation quantifies expiratory dispersion during neonatal resuscitation procedures and compares filters, tube types, and ventilation configurations for controlling exposure to respiratory viruses.

The study benchmarks molecular free-energy calculations for SARS-CoV-2 protease inhibitor binding but does not establish a concrete transfer route to epidemiologic design, surveillance, or population transmission analysis.

The paper proposes snowball sampling for early-outbreak serosurveys and uses simulation to identify conditions under which it estimates asymptomatic infection and seropositivity relationships more efficiently than alternative sampling schemes.
The paper develops an open-source agent-based supermarket model using synthetic movement and shopping data to estimate exposure and compare store-level transmission interventions.
The study uses agent-based scenarios informed by tuberculosis prevalence and health-system data to estimate COVID-19 critical-case demand and capacity pressure in South Africa.
The study reconstructs a documented Jordanian wedding outbreak in an agent-based community model to compare epidemic trajectories with and without strict nonpharmaceutical interventions.
The study calibrates an agent-based model with Vietnamese testing, case, behavioral, policy, and quarantine-breach data to estimate border-reopening rebound risks under alternative responses.
The study combines spatial clustering, environmental correlation, and an agent-based risk-assessment model to map graded risks of human H7N9 infection in China.
The study extends an agent-based COVID-19 model and uses factorial experiments and global sensitivity analysis to quantify individual and combined effects of distancing, masks, and handwashing.
The prospective masked study validates the diagnostic and prognostic accuracy of the RISK11 blood transcriptomic signature for prevalent and incident pulmonary tuberculosis in people with HIV.
The study proposes diagnostic serial interval as a measurable contact-tracing performance indicator and uses agent-based scenarios to relate its timing to epidemic control.
The study uses large-scale agent-based simulations to compare US COVID-19 vaccination plans across vaccine efficacy, uptake, delivery capacity, and nonpharmaceutical-intervention scenarios.
The study develops a random-walk Monte Carlo infection model and evaluates how movement scales and small-world links generate alternative epidemic growth regimes observed across countries.

The study performs a head-to-head diagnostic benchmark of five SARS-CoV-2 antigen tests in asymptomatic specimens, estimating sensitivity, specificity, cycle-threshold performance, and prevalence-dependent predictive values.

The study uses 6,000 farm-level foot-and-mouth-disease outbreak simulations and generalized additive models to compare trigger-based emergency vaccination with fixed, random-start, and stamping-out strategies.
The study develops and validates an agent-based evolving-network method for simulating HIV transmission clusters and evaluating cluster-detection and response strategies.
This molecular docking study proposes therapeutic binders without a transferable epidemiological contribution.
The study proposes a general deterministic and stochastic verification framework for quantifying numerical errors in agent-based models and demonstrates it with a tuberculosis immune-system model.
The study builds, fits, and compares agent-based representations of alternative complex-contagion mechanisms in tuberculosis transmission.
This dynamic agent-based model optimizes COVID-19 vaccine prioritization under limited supply.
The study benchmarks genetic splice-prediction tools for hereditary disorders without an infectious-disease outcome or a concrete infectious-disease transfer route.
The study uses an agent-based transmission model to quantify how vaccination coverage and nonpharmaceutical interventions jointly affect SARS-CoV-2 risk at indoor gatherings.
The study calibrates a microsimulation of shelter transmission to outbreak surveys and compares testing, symptom screening, masking, and combined COVID-19 control strategies.
This agent-based simulation compares standard and delayed second-dose vaccination strategies across uncertain conditions.
This receptor docking study screens therapeutic compounds without a transferable epidemiological method.
The study develops a hybrid agent-based and compartmental evolving-network method for computationally efficient simulation of low-prevalence infectious diseases.
The article reflects on four decades of AIDS and broad lessons for pandemic preparedness without presenting or substantively evaluating a reusable method.
This virtual-screening benchmark supports antiviral drug discovery rather than epidemiological analysis or surveillance.
The study develops and validates DESSABNeT, a discrete-event social-network agent-based model for simulating communicable-disease spread and interventions in large cities.
The study applies a validated agent-based model to quantify the epidemic effects and quarantine burden of alternative SARS-CoV-2 contact-tracing policies in Austria.
This molecular docking study evaluates phytochemical inhibitors without a transferable epidemiological contribution.
This published erratum formally corrects the identified BMJ agent-based study of delayed second-dose COVID-19 vaccination.
The study fits a population pharmacokinetic model from pediatric ceftriaxone concentrations and simulates target attainment to derive dosing recommendations for severe infections.
This molecular docking study identifies therapeutic lead compounds without an epidemiological method contribution.
The study develops an agent-based SEICR simulation and uses designed experiments to estimate how testing and contact tracing affect peak infectious-disease burden.
This nursing-home agent model compares vaccination coverage and screening strategies under uncertain vaccine effects.
The study uses an agent-based model to test how fungal infection could alter ant movement and generate the observed pre-death behavior of infected hosts.
The study combines local airborne-quanta modeling with agent-based behavior to compare distancing, ventilation, class scheduling, and contact tracing for school SARS-CoV-2 control.
This simulation benchmark compares viral-contig detection tools across controlled metagenomic conditions.
The study demonstrates how viral concentration and SARS-CoV-2 mutation accumulation can serve as molecular measures of epidemic phase and containment effectiveness.
The study combines Brownian-dynamics simulation and bacterial experiments to identify polymer-dependent mechanisms and thresholds governing early biofilm compaction and growth.
The study validates qualitative point-of-care antiretroviral drug-level tests and uses pharmacokinetic simulation to determine how reliably they detect recent HIV treatment nonadherence.
The study introduces a generic agent-based framework for constructing stratified infectious-disease scenarios and comparing combinations of public interventions.
This integrated agent-based spatial model optimizes geographic and age-based COVID-19 vaccine allocation.
The study analyzes identifiability under alternative SARS-CoV-2 test-negative designs, derives a novel inverse-probability-weighted estimator, and evaluates it through simulation.

The study uses an agent-based model of visitor behavior and distancing to estimate safe park capacities under alternative contact assumptions, policies, and layouts for contact-transmitted illness control.
This study validates mandatory indication registration as a scalable measurement and benchmarking system for antimicrobial prescribing quality.
This journal cover item briefly advertises another high-resolution COVID-19 modeling article but does not itself add research, methodological, or guidance content.
This molecular docking and dynamics study screens fungal metabolites for antiviral drug development without an epidemiological method contribution.
The study uses an agent-based care-home model to compare how cohorting, visitation, facility size, and community prevalence affect COVID-19 outbreak risk.
The study presents an open-source, age-stratified agent-based COVID-19 model with realistic social networks, intervention modules, statistical-inference support, testing, and documented interfaces.
The study calibrates and validates an agent-based community model to compare full, partial, and virtual school reopening under alternative campus safety protocols.
The study simulates SARS-CoV-2 viral-load, transmissibility, and test-sensitivity processes to identify testing schedules that safely shorten quarantine.

The study compares thirteen clinical viral-metagenomics pipelines on characterized patient datasets, measuring sensitivity, predictive value, low-abundance detection, and implementation characteristics.
The study proposes a mathematical structure for jointly estimating interacting agent-based COVID-19 model parameters and demonstrates its use in evaluating control strategies.

The title suggests demographic benchmarks for equitable COVID-19 vaccination coverage, but the absent abstract leaves insufficient information to determine whether the record develops a substantive measurement method or only advocates a policy goal.
The study simulates insulin stability and diffusion in microneedle polymers for diabetes treatment without an infectious-disease outcome or a concrete infectious-disease transfer route.
The study describes Covasim, a flexible open-source agent-based method integrating demographic structure, layered transmission networks, intrahost dynamics, and configurable COVID-19 interventions.
This pharmacological simulation models cytokine sequestration for combination therapy rather than an epidemiological infectious-process or population method.
The review provides a framework of outcome measures and benchmarks for evaluating and implementing antimicrobial-stewardship programs.
The study develops a multiscale computational-fluid-dynamics simulation linking mask microstructure, leakage, ambient airflow, and infectious-droplet dispersion.
The study uses an agent-based model in fractal city systems to examine how outbreak origin, settlement size, connectivity, and spatial structure alter Ebola and COVID-19 spread.
This study develops and evaluates a Monte Carlo framework for time-dependent infectiousness risk after negative SARS-CoV-2 tests.
The study develops and evaluates a COVID-19 CT-classification workflow that generates synthetic images with a conditional adversarial network and compares classical with quantum machine learning.
The study integrates mobility, census, and building data in a validated spatial agent-based model to compare intervention combinations for preventing COVID-19 resurgence in a megacity.
The paper develops the case time series design, evaluates its validity through simulation, and illustrates its use for influenza-associated myocardial infarction and respiratory symptoms.

This substantive methodological commentary explains how dynamical models represent causal systems and handle interference, spillover, continuous-time feedback, and biological knowledge in infectious-disease epidemiology.
The study uses Monte Carlo simulation with registry and infection-prevalence data to estimate competing-risk-adjusted lifetime gastric-cancer incidence by Helicobacter pylori infection status.
The study constructs an agent-based model calibrated against internet activity to reproduce anti-vaccination dynamics and simulate communication interventions affecting vaccine acceptance.
This agent-based model simulates comparative bat and human immune dynamics during viral infection and evaluates mechanisms of tolerance.
The study applies an animal-disease spread simulation to compare nine stamping-out and vaccination strategies across thirteen foot-and-mouth-disease incursion scenarios.
The study diagnoses medium-dependent bias in microbial colony counts by benchmarking three culture media against whole-genome sequencing and organism-specific qPCR.
This randomized crossover manikin simulation measures how infection-control PPE changes the timing and usability of neonatal resuscitation procedures during a respiratory pandemic.
The study uses model simulation to evaluate a clustered randomized adaptive trial design for sequentially selecting integrated malaria-control interventions.
The paper combines an agent-based MRSA transmission model, contact networks, and sparse colonization observations to develop and validate individual carrier-probability inference.
The paper introduces an agent-based pandemic model with individual viral loads and validates its hospitalization trajectories against COVID-19 data from three New York regions.
The study uses a high-resolution agent-based representation of demographics, mobility, and space to project how vaccination and reopening jointly affect COVID-19 mortality.

The study combines stochastic sample-average optimization with a spatial agent-based spread model, a structure transferable from invasive-pest surveillance to optimizing infectious-vector or pathogen detection networks.
The study uses empirical adolescent contact networks and agent-based simulations to compare random and network-informed classroom cohorting strategies for SARS-CoV-2 control.
The multicenter study demonstrates standardized point-prevalence surveys and WHO AWaRe grouping as a transferable approach for benchmarking pediatric antimicrobial use across hospitals.
Using simulated Ebola outbreaks, the study compares machine-learning imputation methods and quantifies how missingness mechanisms affect case-fatality-ratio bias and precision.
This multicenter in-situ simulation evaluates fit, seal failure, and clinical usability of adapted full-face snorkel masks during aerosol-generating procedures.
This agent-based transmission model compares COVID-19 vaccine prioritization and dosing strategies across policy-relevant outcomes.
The paper creates an expert-labeled CT benchmark dataset and compares convolutional architectures, loss functions, and pretraining strategies for estimating pulmonary COVID-19 involvement.
The review synthesizes immunological, genetic, and epidemiological hypotheses for African COVID-19 patterns but does not develop, compare, or prescribe a transferable epidemiologic method.
The paper combines an agent-based COVID-19 model with genetic algorithms to search over time-varying vaccination allocations for policy decision support.
The study applies Monte Carlo reverse quantitative microbial risk assessment to translate alternative infection-risk benchmarks into acceptable exposure times for wastewater bioaerosols.
The paper develops a full-population agent-based COVID-19 model with parallel collision processing and uses it to explore distancing, lockdown, and vaccination scenarios in Lombardy.
The study reports sustained virologic response after direct-acting antiviral treatment in a patient cohort without identifying a reusable design, model, or measurement contribution.
This methodological review describes an integrated agent-based epidemic-simulation framework spanning synthetic populations, contact-network construction, disease evolution, and policy analysis.
The paper extends the Covasim agent-based simulator to Polish demographic and social conditions for comparing regional combinations of interventions, testing, and vaccination.
The simulation evaluates the mechanical retention of side-by-side Foley balloons for labor induction, and the abstract supplies no concrete route to infectious-disease epidemiologic methods.
The study uses a 17-year environmental monitoring series and percentile distributions to derive operational and resting microbial-air benchmarks for operating theatres.
The molecular-dynamics analysis concerns a hereditary metabolic-enzyme mutation and provides no concrete transfer route to infectious-disease epidemiology.
The study uses an agent-based hospital-evacuation model to compare staffing and segregated-exit strategies under outbreak-specific isolation and infection-control constraints.
The decision-analytic model quantifies racial and ethnic disparities in COVID-19 vaccination and simulates persistent unequal uptake and reduced access barriers.

The study validates a spatially explicit agent-based Taenia solium transmission model by reproducing six interventions from a large cluster-randomized trial and comparing simulated with observed prevalence.
The paper integrates activity-based mobility modeling, mobile-phone data, mechanistic transmission, and individual disease progression into a validated agent-based intervention simulator.
This molecular docking and dynamics study screens microbial compounds for antiviral drug development without an epidemiological method contribution.
The paper develops an agent-based epidemic model that links culturally influenced behavioral decisions and epidemic dynamics using planned-behavior theory and Bayesian inference.
The agent-based model reproduces the life cycle of polarized opinions in a general online case without specifying an infection, vaccination, or concrete infectious-disease transfer route.
This atomistic simulation explains coronavirus protease-protein binding at molecular scale without modeling epidemiological transmission, surveillance, or population outcomes.
The study uses standardized simulated-patient encounters to measure inappropriate antibiotic dispensing for two self-limiting infection scenarios in community pharmacies.
This journal article reports the same indoor SARS-CoV-2 agent-based intervention study as the supplied earlier preprint record.
The paper develops a space-time agent-based aerosol exposure method and compares its heterogeneous infection-risk estimates with those from the classical well-mixed Wells-Riley model.
This veterinary immunophenotyping feasibility study measures host responses rather than infectious epidemiology.
The study develops a spatial African swine fever transmission simulation to estimate how distance, roads, rivers, and fences alter spread among wild-boar habitats.

The study combines Wells-Riley risk calculations with agent-based simulation to assess whether community testing centers can screen residents within exposure-time limits that constrain cross-infection.
This virtual screening and molecular dynamics study identifies influenza polymerase inhibitors for drug development without an epidemiological contribution.
This computational vaccine-construction study lacks a transferable epidemiological method or infectious-process model.
This calibrated agent-based transmission model compares vaccine prioritization strategies and their effects on infections, hospitalizations, and deaths.
This Monte Carlo model quantifies population benefits and uncertain adverse-event risks of extending HPV vaccination to boys.

The paper develops a machine-learning-assisted parameter-identification procedure for calibrating the Covasim agent-based model and validates it with COVID-19 data from New York and the United Kingdom.

The observational cohort reports neutralizing-antibody trajectories and standard regression associations with demographic and clinical characteristics without a distinct transferable methodological contribution.
The study uses repeated cross-sectional surveys over sixteen years to benchmark changes in parasite-control practices, monitoring, treatment, and resistance testing in Australian sheep production.
